ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for vegetarian kway teow; mapped to the broader 'noodle dish' class as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (rice noodles, stir-fried) used as the closest defensible match; no dedicated code exists for the vegetarian variant with mock meat.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific prepared dish; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker-centre plate of vegetarian kway teow weighs approximately 300 g, representing a typical single-person lunch or dinner serving in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Vegetarian Kway Teow — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Vegetarian Kway Teow (cr_id F06010004). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is not reported by HPB for this entry and is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for comparable stir-fried rice noodle dishes (approximately 1.0 g/100 g); this estimation is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — rice noodles, stir-fried (analogous estimate)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for vegetarian kway teow in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 (medium) is estimated from the closest analogous food: rice noodles, stir-fried, as reported in Atkinson FS et al. (2021) International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values, Diabetes Care. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 27.8 × 3.0) / 100 = 54.2 rounded; using per-100g carbohydrate of 27.8 g and a 300 g serving: GL = 65 × (27.8 × 3) / 100 = 65 × 83.4 / 100 = 54.2. Note: input record cited GL of 18.1 which appears to be calculated on a 100 g reference portion (65 × 27.8 / 100 = 18.1); the per-serving GL of 54.2 is reported here for a 300 g plate. The per-100g-reference GL of 18.1 is preserved in notes for cross-reference.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 515 mg per 100 g, driven by dark and light soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid vegetarian kway teow due to its sodium content of 515 mg/100 g (approximately 1,545 mg per standard 300 g plate), which substantially exceeds the recommended single-meal sodium ceiling of 800 mg; if consumed, request reduced soy sauce and avoid adding condiments at the table.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined flat rice noodles with medium-high GI (65) and low dietary fibre (1.21 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should exercise caution with vegetarian kway teow because the refined rice noodles carry a GI of 65 and deliver approximately 83 g of carbohydrate per standard 300 g plate with only 3.6 g of dietary fibre, producing a high glycaemic load that can cause rapid postprandial glucose elevation; portion should be limited to approximately 150 g and paired with a high-fibre or high-protein side dish to blunt the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (515 mg/100 g) and moderate fat (5.28 g/100 g) with estimated saturated fat ~1.0 g/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease should consume vegetarian kway teow sparingly due to its high sodium content (515 mg/100 g), which contributes to blood pressure elevation, and its moderate fat content; the mock meat component provides plant-based protein which is cardioprotective, but the overall sodium load makes this dish unsuitable for frequent consumption without modification.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium 515 mg/100 g exceeds RA threshold; refined carbohydrates contribute to inflammatory glycaemic load; chili is a nightshade ingredient
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit vegetarian kway teow due to sodium at 515 mg/100 g worsening RA-related tissue swelling, the pro-inflammatory glycaemic load from refined rice noodles, and the presence of chili (a nightshade) which may aggravate RA flares in sensitive individuals; request reduced soy sauce and monitor personal tolerance to nightshade ingredients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vegetarian Kway Teow (素炒粿條) is a popular Singaporean hawker dish consisting of broad, flat rice noodles stir-fried at high heat with mock meat (typically soy-based textured protein), bean sprouts, chye sim (Chinese flowering cabbage), garlic, and a combination of dark and light soy sauce. The dish is the vegetarian adaptation of the classic char kway teow, replacing pork lard, Chinese sausage, and cockles with plant-based alternatives. The noodles are silky and slightly chewy, with a savoury, umami-rich flavour profile derived from the soy sauces and wok hei (breath of the wok). Per 100 g, the dish provides 184 kcal, 6.19 g protein, 5.28 g fat, and 27.8 g carbohydrate with 1.21 g dietary fibre. Sodium is notably high at 515 mg/100 g, making it a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. The glycaemic index is estimated at 65 (medium), driven by the refined rice noodle base, and the glycaemic load per standard 300 g plate is approximately 54, making it a high-GL meal for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The mock meat contributes plant-based protein and iron (1.16 mg/100 g). This dish is suitable for vegetarians but is not vegan (some mock meat products contain egg) and is not gluten-free due to soy sauce. It is commonly consumed as a working lunch or dinner at Singapore hawker centres and food courts.
common_user_queries: How many calories in vegetarian kway teow?
Is vegetarian kway teow suitable for diabetics?
Can I eat vegetarian kway teow if I have high blood pressure?
What is the difference between char kway teow and vegetarian kway teow?
How much sodium is in vegetarian kway teow?
What is mock meat in kway teow?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and secondary nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F06010004, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA SR Legacy analogous data and documented. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson 2021 peer-reviewed tables, which is the approved GI source. All other fields are derived from these approved sources or are documented as estimated.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.0 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for comparable stir-fried rice noodle dishes, as HPB does not report saturated fat for this entry. This is an approximation and should be updated when HPB publishes detailed fatty acid data. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific vegetarian kway teow preparation are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026. 3. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on stir-fried rice noodles from Atkinson FS et al. (2021) International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values, Diabetes Care; no direct measurement for vegetarian kway teow exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. The per-100 g reference GL of 18.1 (as reported in the input record) is consistent with this GI estimate (65 × 27.8 / 100 = 18.07). The per-serving GL of 54.2 is calculated for a standard 300 g plate. 4. Dietary flags: is_vegan set to false because some commercially available mock meat products used in hawker-centre preparations contain egg white; consumers should verify with the vendor. is_halal set to false as halal certification varies by stall and mock meat supplier. 5. FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are approximate matches; no exact ontology codes exist for this specific dish.
